title: Application of Discrete Mathematics principles in Algorithms.

title: My Introduction to Kubernetes.
summary: This is a tool for orchestrating containers in a containerized application running on one or many machines on the cloud or in hybrid situations. These containers are usually Docker containers, but Kubernetes can manage any container that follows the Open Container Initiative.
code:
const{Fragment:e,jsx:t,jsxs:n}=arguments[0];function _createMdxContent(a){const o={li:"li",nav:"nav",ol:"ol",p:"p",strong:"strong",ul:"ul",...a.components},{Img:i}=o;return i||function(e,t){throw new Error("Expected "+(t?"component":"object")+" `"+e+"` to be defined: you likely forgot to import, pass, or provide it.")}("Img",!0),n(e,{children:[t(o.nav,{className:"toc",children:t(o.ol,{className:"toc-level toc-level-1"})}),t(o.p,{children:t(o.strong,{children:"My Introduction to Kubernetes."})}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"This is a tool for orchestrating containers in a containerized application running on one or many machines on the cloud or in hybrid situations. These containers are usually Docker containers, but Kubernetes can manage any container that follows the Open Container Initiative. A container is a standard unit of software where the code is packaged with all its dependencies and requirements so that the application runs smoothly even when the software is running in different environments."}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"As more applications became global, more companies adopted the microservices architecture instead of the monolithic architecture. This meant that there was a growing need for an orchestration tool to manage these increasingly complicated software architectures. Kubernetes was created by Google to solve that problem. Later, the application was open-sourced, where it quickly became one of the most popular container orchestration tools."}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"I like to think of a Kubernetes cluster as an orchestra that needs to follow the musical notes to create beautiful music, and Kubernetes is the orchestrator guiding each musician to play their parts in a way based on what is required, as well as adapting to any changes that may happen. You might think that as long as each musician can just practice their part and execute a predefined plan, all would be well. But things can change. Quickly. So, having a master organizer making sure the whole group can work together to perform music to a high standard is crucial."}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"For example, you could have a full-stack application called ShopSmart, which has a frontend web app, backend services, and a database. All these parts of the whole could be within a container along with the required dependencies. The container would be made to run on either a virtual or local machine."}),"
",t(i,{src:"/media/blog/introduction-to-kubernetes/image2.png",alt:"",width:411,height:476,className:"mx-auto h-auto max-h-[476px] w-auto max-w-full object-contain",blurDataURL:"data:image/webp;base64,UklGRiQAAABXRUJQVlA4IBgAAAAwAQCdASoIAAkADMDOJaQAA3AA/uo6EAA=",placeholder:"blur"}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"During development, it is likely that all that would be required would be a single container to handle all requests from only a handful of users. The infrastructure would easily handle the current load.
After the launch of the full-stack application, the number of requests from users rapidly rises, and soon, the pod struggles to handle the large amount of load."}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"So you adapt to the rising load by horizontally scaling your application by increasing the number of replicas of your containers and connecting them to a load balancer so that the requests are shared among them efficiently."}),"
",t(i,{src:"/media/blog/introduction-to-kubernetes/image5.png",alt:"",width:624,height:411,className:"h-auto max-w-full object-contain",blurDataURL:"data:image/webp;base64,UklGRiQAAABXRUJQVlA4IBgAAAAwAQCdASoIAAUADMDOJaQAA3AA/uqSAAA=",placeholder:"blur"}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"The infrastructure does get more complex and difficult to maintain, but at a reasonable load, it’s manageable. But then your e-commerce platform becomes extremely successful, and millions, if not billions, of users want to use it daily. At that sort of scale, it is hard to imagine how any company manages all that easily. A company could hire a large number of DevOps professionals, but crashes would be likely due to human error. Kubernetes helps solve this problem."}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"A Kubernetes cluster ( which is a single Kubernetes system unit) consists of a master node and many worker nodes."}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"Kubernetes can:"}),"
",n(o.ul,{children:["
",t(o.li,{children:"Act as a load balancer by using Services to distribute load across different nodes."}),"
",t(o.li,{children:"Act as an autoscaler that, based on defined rules, can increase or decrease the number of nodes running based on current load requirements."}),"
"]}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"Kubernetes is designed to be self-healing. It ensures a desired state of applications by restarting failed containers, replacing pods, and scheduling them on healthy nodes"}),"
",t(i,{src:"/media/blog/introduction-to-kubernetes/image1.png",alt:"",width:624,height:600,className:"h-auto max-w-full object-contain",blurDataURL:"data:image/webp;base64,UklGRiYAAABXRUJQVlA4IBoAAAAwAQCdASoIAAgADMDOJZQAA3AA/upJjpwIAA==",placeholder:"blur"}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"A node can be thought of as an abstraction over a physical or virtual machine that serves as a worker in a cluster, responsible for running containers. A node contains one or more pods. A pod is the smallest, most basic deployment unit in a cluster that acts as a wrapper over a containerized application."}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"You can have multiple master nodes in a cluster to ensure that one is always available in case there is a failure. The master node, also known as a control plane, contains some important components."}),"
",n(o.ul,{children:["
",n(o.li,{children:["
",n(o.p,{children:["The ",t(o.strong,{children:"API Server"})," is the module that receives requests from the operator through API, CLI, or SDK and processes the request accordingly. The kubelet(node agent) constantly communicates with the API Server, sending health status reports, node registration, pod assignment, and status updates."]}),"
"]}),"
",n(o.li,{children:["
",n(o.p,{children:["The ",t(o.strong,{children:"Scheduler"})," is responsible for scheduling the placement of containers on different nodes based on the required load and the available resources on the worker nodes."]}),"
"]}),"
",n(o.li,{children:["
",n(o.p,{children:["The ",t(o.strong,{children:"Controller Manager"})," is vital for maintaining the operational integrity of the cluster. It ensures the cluster remains desired even during failures. It automatically handles the scaling of resources to ensure the workloads can adapt to changing demands."]}),"
"]}),"
",n(o.li,{children:["
",n(o.p,{children:[t(o.strong,{children:"ETCD"})," Store can be thought of as the brain of the cluster. It stores the current state of the application along with all the configuration data for all parts of the cluster. It enables backup and restore as the state of the application is saved to the store as snapshots."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"So, what would the Kubernetes cluster for our application look like then?"}),"
",t(i,{src:"/media/blog/introduction-to-kubernetes/image3.png",alt:"",width:624,height:503,className:"h-auto max-w-full object-contain",blurDataURL:"data:image/webp;base64,UklGRigAAABXRUJQVlA4IBwAAABwAQCdASoIAAYADMDOJZQC7AF1AAD+6UTKzwAA",placeholder:"blur"}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"Now our architecture is being run in a cluster with a number of nodes, with a number of pods within them. The frontend and backend are being run in a single pod, which can be replicated depending on changing demands. But the database has been moved to its own separate pod. There is a sensible explanation for this. It is recommended that modules of the app should be in a pod only if they are:"}),"
",n(o.ul,{children:["
",t(o.li,{children:"Tightly coupled"}),"
",t(o.li,{children:"Share a volume of data"}),"
",t(o.li,{children:"their lifecycles are tied together"}),"
",t(o.li,{children:"Share the same localhost"}),"
"]}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"At this moment, it is better that the DB is not running in the same pod as the other modules. As the DB is the most critical aspect of the app. It works with access to the volume that stores the app data, and this volume is not needed by the other modules. The lifecycle of the other modules is significantly different from that of the DB container, as the database needs to have high availability. The database is stateful, so it is important that it has its own container and pod.
You could even adjust the configuration of the deployments even further by considering more factors."}),"
",n(o.ul,{children:["
",n(o.li,{children:["
",n(o.p,{children:[t(o.strong,{children:"Independent scaling"}),": The DB containers will need to be replicated based on the amount of database read/ writes required, and this means it has to be scaled differently from the app. Additionally, the frontend application usually does not require as much memory and CPU cycles as the backend, so it may not make sense to scale these modules the same. To save on resources, the two modules should be in separate pods."]}),"
"]}),"
",n(o.li,{children:["
",n(o.p,{children:[t(o.strong,{children:"Independent deployments"}),": The frontend changes frequently, CSS rewrites/ HTML adjustments. Compare that with the backend, which changes a lot more infrequently. The DB would rarely be changed as the database needs to be highly available. The DB should not have to be restarted every time the frontend changes."]}),"
"]}),"
",n(o.li,{children:["
",n(o.p,{children:[t(o.strong,{children:"Fault isolation"}),": If the backend crashes, the pod becomes unhealthy, so the frontend is restarted as well. Meaning the whole site would be available. At least the frontend could provide UI until the backend is back online. The same goes for the DB."]}),"
"]}),"
",n(o.li,{children:["
",n(o.p,{children:[t(o.strong,{children:"Separate security policies:"})," The frontend serves static HTML, some CSS, and JavaScript. While the backend makes use of confidential secrets, if the pod is compromised from the frontend, it could be disastrous for the business if the backend and DB are accessed as well."]}),"
"]}),"
"]}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"Now that we have considered more factors. The architecture would look more like this."}),"
",t(i,{src:"/media/blog/introduction-to-kubernetes/image4.png",alt:"",width:624,height:620,className:"h-auto max-w-full object-contain",blurDataURL:"data:image/webp;base64,UklGRigAAABXRUJQVlA4IBwAAABwAQCdASoIAAgADMDOJZQC/OF1AAD+6aRYdgAA",placeholder:"blur"}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"These are just ways the cluster can be adjusted based on changing considerations."}),"
",t(o.p,{children:"While Kubernetes gives us the tools to build more complex, robust, and scalable applications, it is still important to use our intuition when designing these systems. Also, not every application may need that much complexity. There is no need to rush towards a microservices infrastructure if your business does not require it.
Added complexity means more resources and cost, and also a greater possibility for human error. A good understanding of the business requirements and how to apply that to system design is as important as the technical knowledge for a DevOps specialist."})]})}return{default:function(e={}){const{wrapper:n}=e.components||{};return n?t(n,{...e,children:t(_createMdxContent,{...e})}):_createMdxContent(e)}};

title: More on Kubernetes.
summary: So I decided to get right into working with Kubernetes because that does help with learning how to operate a cluster. It also gives context for further study.

const{Fragment:e,jsx:n,jsxs:t}=arguments[0];function _createMdxContent(o){const a={a:"a",code:"code",nav:"nav",ol:"ol",p:"p",pre:"pre",strong:"strong",...o.components},{Img:r}=a;return r||function(e,n){throw new Error("Expected "+(n?"component":"object")+" `"+e+"` to be defined: you likely forgot to import, pass, or provide it.")}("Img",!0),t(e,{children:[n(a.nav,{className:"toc",children:n(a.ol,{className:"toc-level toc-level-1"})}),n(a.p,{children:n(a.strong,{children:"More on Kubernetes."})}),"
",n(a.p,{children:"So I decided to get right into working with Kubernetes because that does help with learning how to operate a cluster. It also gives context for further study. Before that, it is important to discuss some crucial components of a Kubernetes cluster."}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["These components are ",n(a.strong,{children:"Services"}),", ",n(a.strong,{children:"Ingress"}),", ",n(a.strong,{children:"ConfigMap"}),", ",n(a.strong,{children:"Secrets"}),", ",n(a.strong,{children:"StatefulSet"}),", ",n(a.strong,{children:"Volume"}),", and ",n(a.strong,{children:"Deployments"}),"."]}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["Every pod gets an IP address. Pods are ephemeral and can die quickly. If a pod dies, say, because the container crashes due to lack of resources, it would be recreated with a different IP address. That would be annoying to manage if you had other components that talk to that pod. So you use a ",n(a.strong,{children:"Service"}),". A service has its own IP address, and it can act like a proxy for the pods. The lifecycle of the pods and the service differs, so the service’s IP address won’t change if the pod is recreated."]}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["Say you want the application to be available through a browser. You can create an internal service that you can use to connect one component to another within your application (e.g, a database), but have it not connect to the outside world. You can use an external service to have users connect to certain components of your cluster, say, the front-end application, but the address would look like 102.168.11.0:8080, which would make you seem like a scammer. So you need to use ",n(a.strong,{children:"Ingress"}),", which would have a more readable address and forward whichever request is sent to it to the appropriate destination. The service also acts as a load balancer, diverting traffic to an appropriate pod based on changing demands and available resources."]}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["A ",n(a.strong,{children:"ConfigMap"})," acts as a store for configuration data useful for running the application. It is an external configuration for your Kubernetes cluster. Putting credentials into a ConfigMap in plain text is not advisable, though, but you can store these in ",n(a.strong,{children:"Secrets"}),". That does not mean that information is automatically secure in the secret, though. It is expected that the data is encrypted by a third party. A Secret is an external store for your application, specifically designed to store confidential information."]}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["Remember when I said pods are ephemeral. What about the pod that holds your database? That would be inefficient as the data stored could be lost easily. You can use your ",n(a.strong,{children:"Volume"}),", a persistent way to manage data storage. A Volume is a directory that is accessible to all containers in a pod, providing a shared data storage that can survive container crashes. You can configure the data storage to be stored locally in the machine or in a remote location. That is not part of the cluster, but you can use a reference to it. Kubernetes does not manage data persistence. A database cannot be replicated with a deployment, as databases have states. So you need to ensure that the different clones of the database access the same storage and also manage which pods are writing to the storage and reading from the storage in order to avoid data inconsistencies. The controller that enables this is called a ",n(a.strong,{children:"StatefulSet"}),". StatefulSet is a controller that manages applications requiring persistent storage, stable network identities, and ordered deployment/scaling. It is used specifically for stateful applications. It allows for replication. It makes sure the database reads and writes asynchronously so that data inconsistencies are avoided."]}),"
",n(a.p,{children:n(a.strong,{children:"Creating a local cluster"})}),"
",n(a.p,{children:"A good handle on operating a machine with Bash is very helpful when working with Kubernetes, as a fair number of nodes run Linux as their OS. You could use others, but Bash is usually your best bet, especially if you plan to attend a CKA or CKAD exam. Now, to practice, you do need a virtual node or a virtual machine, a virtual machine on your local machine. There are, I know of three options to get them easily."}),"
",n(a.p,{children:"We will be writing to files using bash. If you do not have bash on your computer, make sure to find another alternative, such as WSL, CMD, or similar. Or you can install git bash, which should work similarly."}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["We will begin by installing a very useful command-line tool called kubectl. This can be installed by following the instructions ",n(a.a,{href:"https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/",children:"here"}),". This tool has many commands that we can use to easily interact with the cluster."]}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["Docker or similar is required for running Kubernetes clusters, as they are the technology responsible for running the containers. Follow the instructions in the links for ",n(a.a,{href:"https://docs.docker.com/desktop/setup/install/mac-install/",children:"Mac"}),", ",n(a.a,{href:"https://docs.docker.com/desktop/setup/install/windows-install/",children:"Windows"}),", or ",n(a.a,{href:"https://docs.docker.com/desktop/setup/install/linux/",children:"Linux"}),". If you have successfully installed it, run docker version in your terminal to confirm. Open Docker Desktop and agree to the terms. Choose recommended settings and click Finish. Then you should be done."]}),"
",n(a.p,{children:"Now we need a virtual machine that will host our clusters. Thankfully, there are some open source options to easily host a Kubernetes cluster on our machine."}),"
",n(a.p,{children:n(a.strong,{children:"The Docker route:"})}),"
",n(a.p,{children:"Docker gives us the ability to easily create a cluster and get started operating with the click of a button. Just open Docker Desktop, navigate to the Kubernetes page by clicking on the word in the sidebar. You should see a create button. Click it. And you are done."}),"
",n(a.p,{children:n(a.strong,{children:"MiniKube"})}),"
",n(a.p,{children:"This is a local Kubernetes cluster that enables you to learn and develop for Kubernetes. All you need is Docker (or similarly compatible) container or a Virtual Machine environment."}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["Follow the instructions ",n(a.a,{href:"https://minikube.sigs.k8s.io/docs/start",children:"here"})," to get started. After run:"]}),"
",n(a.p,{children:n(a.code,{children:"minikube start"})}),"
",n(a.p,{children:n(a.strong,{children:"Rauncher Desktop"})}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["If the other two give you issues, there is a third option I found that makes things easy to set up and manage Kubernetes on my device. It is an open-source application that provides all the essentials to work with containers and Kubernetes on the desktop. You can download an installer ",n(a.a,{href:"https://rancherdesktop.io/",children:"here"}),". During installation, make sure DockerD and Kubernetes are enabled."]}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["Lastly, you need a popular command tool for interacting with our Kubernetes clusters called kubectl. You can install through this ",n(a.a,{href:"https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/",children:"link"}),"."]}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["If you managed to get one of these configurations done, you should see some options when you run
",n(a.code,{children:"kubectl config get-contexts"})]}),"
",t(a.p,{children:["You can choose which cluster you wish to use by running:|
",n(a.code,{children:"kubectl config use-context <cluster_name>"})]}),"
",n(a.p,{children:"Now, create a new folder in your workspace with your project name. It is within this folder that we will keep all our deployment and configuration files."}),"
",n(a.p,{children:n(a.strong,{children:"Web application Demo"})}),"
",n(r,{src:"/media/blog/more-on-kubernetes/image1.png",alt:"",width:1999,height:1314,className:"mx-auto h-auto max-h-[520px] w-auto max-w-full object-contain",blurDataURL:"data:image/webp;base64,UklGRiQAAABXRUJQVlA4IBgAAAAwAQCdASoIAAUADMDOJZwAA3AA/unMAAA=",placeholder:"blur"}),"
",n(a.p,{children:"This section includes instructions to produce a simple cluster which host a web application that can be accessed from the browser. This will involve creating two deployments, one that will host the web app, which should connect to a pod created from a deployment for a MongoDB database. The web app will require configuration data, which it will get from a ConfigMap, and the mongo-user and mongo-password will be stored in a Secret. Both of these we will configure."}),"
",n(a.p,{children:n(a.strong,{children:"Creating our ConfigMap"})}),"
",n(a.p,{children:"We will now create our ConfigMap by defining it in YAML format. Run nano mongo-config.yaml and input the following text:"}),"
",n(a.pre,{children:n(a.code,{children:"apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata
  name: mongo-config
data:
  mongo-url: mongo-service
"})}),"
",n(a.p,{children:"This is the configuration for the ConfigMap. Set the type of component in the kind value. Metadata is a key value object that the user can use to label the configmap with any tag they want. Set the configuration data values in the data object. This is also a key-value store. Mongo-service is the URL of a service that will be created for the MongoDB. Create the config map by running:"}),"
",n(a.p,{children:n(a.code,{children:"kubectl apply -f mongo-config.yaml"})}),"
